attributes.stem: A 19-year-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-year history of facial acne. He has type 1 diabetes mellitus and hayfever, and has an insulin pump. He has allergies to animal dander. He reports washing his face five times daily with soap and hot water to try to improve the condition. He works in a boiler factory. On examination, he appears well with vital signs within normal limits. His skin is slightly erythematous and appears dry, with a few inflammatory papules on his forehead and cheeks, but no scarring, nodules or cysts.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate lifestyle advice?
options: Apply alcohol-based toner three times daily
Wash face with soap and water every two hours
Gentle cleansing twice daily with lukewarm water
Use facial scrub daily to remove dead skin
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with mild acne and is currently over-cleansing his face, which can lead to skin irritation and potentially worsen acne. Over-washing can strip the skin of natural oils, leading to increased oil production as a compensatory mechanism. There are multiple skin care products on the market which make it confusing for patients to know what to use. The most appropriate lifestyle advice focuses on gentle skincare practices that maintain the skin barrier while managing acne.

Gentle cleansing twice daily with lukewarm water and a mild cleanser is the recommended approach. This maintains adequate hygiene while avoiding excessive drying and irritation of the skin.

Avoiding all dairy products is an extreme measure not supported by strong evidence. While some studies suggest a link between dairy consumption, high glyceamic index foods and acne, complete elimination is unnecessary and may impact nutrition in adolescents. It is not an allergy or related to being an atopic individual.

Washing face with soap and water every two hours represents excessive cleansing that will likely damage the skin barrier and exacerbate inflammation. This can lead to increased redness, irritation, and potentially worsen acne.

Applying alcohol-based toner three times daily would be too harsh and drying for the skin. Alcohol-based products can strip the skin of natural oils and cause irritation, potentially leading to increased oil production and inflammation.

Using facial scrub daily is too aggressive for acne-prone skin. Physical exfoliants can cause micro-tears in the skin and spread bacteria, potentially worsening inflammation and acne lesions.

Additional lifestyle advice for acne management includes using non-comedogenic light moisturisers and sunscreen, avoiding picking or squeezing lesions, maintaining a healthy diet and avoiding steamy or hot excessive exposure (as in a boiler factory though not feasible to change occupations). If these measures are insufficient, topical treatments such as benzoyl peroxide or retinoids (adapalene) may be considered in his case. Acne vulgaris can cause significant psychological impact on vulnerable teenagers, and intervention can always be done even if it takes time for improvement.
learningPoints: First-line management of mild acne includes gentle skin cleansing and non-comedogenic moisturisers.
Diet modification has limited evidence in acne management, though some studies suggest dairy and high glycaemic index foods may worsen acne.
Acne is not caused by being dirty thus excessive washing is drying and unproductive.
references: Cook D, Krassas G, Huang T. Acne: Best Practice Management. Australian Family Physician. 2010: 39 (9). Available at: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/41d41870-eba3-46c6-a7bf-26f2a3e2905f/Acne-best-practice-management.aspx

Reynolds R et al. Guidelines of care for the management of acne vulgaris. Journal of American Academy of Dermatology. 2024: 90 (5). Available at: https://www.jaad.org/article/S0190-9622(23)03389-3/fulltext
